responses: Cheyenne Wyoming off and on 10 years owned property here 20 years

My campaign Slogan is The youngest us postal clerk in the country proudly served the Navajo reservation and community George’s Hogan Thoreau New Mexico I ran my grandma’s Post office in her store pawnshop and gasoline station and Sub station post office of 300 PO Boxes I started at the age of 6 my reading abilities were middle school level as far as a political candidate I will do what is right and walk with the Bible is my cloak and shield and the Ten Commandments are my daggers to fight evil
Is growth and prosperity for business and all
The more jobs add to the economy what can be done is lower tax rates the more taxes are lowered we can cap health care to compete which is lower the cost
Very careful I don’t want to see land go unless it is absolutely necessary to benefit the citizens and and wildlife
Growth the more we prosper is jobs this lowers the cost of everything

Cheyenne, Wyoming. I have lived here for three years. I am a Wyoming native and grew up in Farson, Wyoming. I was born in Rock Springs, WY. 
My Wyoming roots run deep. Raised in Farson, I have an appreciation for hard work, independence, and service. My experience in business, community engagement, and policy shaped my belief that our future should be built by the people who live, work, and raise families here. I am proud of the Wyoming way of life and I believe our families want safe communities, strong economies, protected freedoms, and conservative leaders who will fight for their values. I am running for Wyoming State Senate to bring principled, conservative leadership. As a wife, mother, and community leader, I understand the values that make our state strong: faith, family, freedom, and a deep respect for the people who call Wyoming home. I believe we deserves leaders who listen, stand firm in their values, and work collaboratively to get things done. I am committed to protecting our freedoms, defending constitutional rights, supporting local communities, and ensuring state government works for the people it serves.
Wyoming shaped me. Growing up in here, I learned hard work, self-reliance, and what it means to be part of a community. That was my Wyoming — and I want it to still be Wyoming when my daughter grows up.
Keeping Wyoming strong for our youth means protecting their constitutional freedoms. It means keeping parents in charge of their children's education, not bureaucrats. It means building an economy where they can actually stay — protecting our energy industry, growing opportunity, and making housing affordable so our kids don't have to leave the state they love.
When I look at my daughter, I see every Wyoming kid who deserves to grow up free, safe, and full of possibility. That is what I am fighting for.
Wyoming is a big state with small towns, and healthcare access is a real challenge. Here is how I would address it.
Expand telehealth so rural families can access quality care without driving hours to see a provider. Grow our healthcare workforce by continuing to build out programs and capacity at our colleges and creating support to recruit and retain providers across the state. Protect our hospitals — they are lifelines for small Wyoming communities and state policy should support them, not burden them. And increase competition and transparency so patients have options and can afford the care they need.
Healthcare solutions that work elsewhere don’t work for Wyoming. Our communities should have the flexibility to address their unique needs without one-size-fits-all mandates from the federal government.
Accessible healthcare isn't about expanding government — it's about removing barriers so Wyoming families can get the care they need, close to home, at a price they can afford.
I do not. Wyoming's public lands are part of who we are — where we hunt, fish, ranch, and make our living. Keeping them public means every Wyoming family has access to the land that defines this state.
But keeping land public is not enough. Federal lands require proper management and investment, and that means Wyoming needs a real seat at the table. Too often, decisions about our land are made by people in Washington who have never set foot here.
I believe in collaboration — where Wyoming's ranchers, energy producers, sportsmen, and local communities have a direct voice in how our federal lands are managed. We know this land. We depend on it. We are its best stewards.
Managed well, public lands support grazing, energy, recreation, and tourism — all of which matter deeply to Wyoming families. The goal is not less management. It is smarter, locally informed management that reflects Wyoming's values.
Wyoming's tax structure needs more than quick fixes — it needs a long-term, comprehensive review that looks at the full picture.
I support a measured approach that does two things at once: delivers real relief to Wyoming families and businesses, and ensures our state can still fund the services people depend on. Those goals are not in conflict — but achieving both requires honest, thorough work rather than reactive legislation.
Wyoming has always been a low-tax state and I intend to keep it that way. Smart tax policy is not just about cutting — it is about making sure every dollar collected is spent wisely and the burden is distributed fairly.
Wyoming families and businesses deserve a tax system they can understand, predict, and trust. That starts with a serious, data-driven review and a commitment to reform that puts people first.
The legislature's role in economic development is to create conditions where Wyoming businesses can grow and thrive. That means smart infrastructure investment, a predictable regulatory environment, and support that empowers communities.
The Wyoming Business Council needs to evolve into a clear, outcome-driven model with defined goals, measurable results, and real accountability. This work must be built from the ground up. Local communities know their assets and gaps better than anyone in Cheyenne.
Workforce development is a key driver this conversation, which is what I work toward daily in my career. Businesses cannot grow without people, and people cannot stay without opportunity. Infrastructure — roads, broadband, water, and energy — is the foundation that makes all of it possible.
Wyoming deserves leadership that sets clear priorities, tracks outcomes, and works hand in hand with local communities to build an economy built to last.
Campaign donations are an investment in the democratic process — nothing more, nothing less. When someone donates to my campaign, they are helping fund the practical work of running for office: yard signs, mailers, events, and the tools needed to reach voters across Senate District 5. That is what donations do. They do not buy influence, they do not guarantee alignment, and they absolutely do not purchase a vote.
I answer to the people of Senate District 5 — all of them — not to any donor, organization, or outside interest. Every decision I make in the State Senate will be guided by what is right for my constituents and for Wyoming.
Wyoming's campaign laws are not stringent like other states, and I hope we can continue on a process that keeps government out of the way. CheckGate challenges Wyoming's values, but I do not believe we need more regulation, we need Wyoming voters to come to the polls on August 18th. That you for your consideration and support!

I live at 808 West Dale Blvd. in Cheyenne. I have lived here since 2008. I have lived in Wyoming all of my life, except for a short stint in Denver from 1981-1982 and Anchorage in 1990. I was born in Cheyenne, Wyoming.

I graduated from Cheyenne East High School in 1997. When I was growing up, my family had a small ranch south of Encampment Wyoming. I spent every weekend and summer there. That is where I developed my love of the mountains. The ranch borders Medicine Bow National Forest, which was a very big back yard! I received a BS in Chemical Engineering from the University of Wyoming. I worked in the oil business for 10 years and for a chemical manufacturing company for 12. I worked for several small consulting companies before starting my own consulting company, Hanlon Process Consulting, LLC, in 2013. My background gives me unique insight into the industries that drive Wyoming's  economy. As a consultant I helped multiple companies operate more safely, efficiently, and profitably. The state of Wyoming needs the same kind of support.

We are challenged by a constitutional mandate and supreme court rulings to fund a quality public education.Developing the holistic funding and oversight mechanisms to ensure that we provide every child with the best education possible without a contentious and divisive disagreement every year is a challenge I am ready for.

Wyoming has the opportunity to become the "power house" for the rest of the country. We need to listen to the people who live and work here and develop the resources with Wyoming jobs and quality of life as our primary objectives. We can do that. The challenge is to develop those resources in a way that allows our children and grandchildren to stay here because they have outstanding job opportunites and a way that causes them to want to stay here because of the quality of life.

We can take advantage of the federal support available to us to maximize coverage and reduce premium costs. Wyomingites have paid the federal taxes that fund these programs. It is foolish to reject the funding and make life difficult for so many Wyoming families. We can work with Wyoming insurance companies to establish creative programs to reduce their risk and pass that savings on to Wyoming people. Those are easy steps that will require very little state funding.

When we take those necessary steps, there will still be working Wyoming families that can't afford their health insurance premiums. It is a more difficult and contentious task to create programs to ensure all Wyomingites can afford their health insurance but the resources are available. If we have the will, we can provide affordable insurance to everyone who wants insurance.

Everyone in Wyoming (with the possible exception of a few politicians who have been influenced by East Coast agendas) opposes the sale or transfer of public lands. No other issue brings us all together as one community in the same way. I stand with all my friends and neighbors who want to keep public lands in public hands!

To manage the lands in the best interests of the people of Wyoming, we must ensure that management practices are science-based and that the agencies that manage state and federal public lands have the financial resources to manage the lands well. At the same time, we should ensure that we take maximum advantage of new technology to reduce management costs.
The legislature must ensure that the tax structure developed by the state has a high enough "ceiling" that each local government can provide the services that their constituents require.

The purpose of government is to secure collective stability, protect individual rights, and manage systemic risks, creating a predictable framework where people can safely cooperate and prosper. If the legislature takes that purpose seriously, they will always have a central role in economic development.

The system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures seems to work pretty well. It would be nice if all campaigns were funded the same, but that isn't the way the world works and it certainly isn't something that I have the power to change.

questions: Where do you live currently? How long have you lived there? How long have you lived in Wyoming? Where were you born?
What age will you be on Election Day, Nov. 3, 2026?
Please tell the voters about yourself including your background and qualifications.
What are the biggest challenges and opportunities facing Wyoming today? As a legislator, how would you address them?

We asked WyoFile readers to rank issues that are important to them, and healthcare costs and access topped the list. What can the Legislature do to make healthcare more affordable and accessible to Wyomingites?
Do you support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming? How do you plan to ensure that state and federal land is managed in the best interest of the people of Wyoming?
No two Wyoming counties are the same when it comes to how they generate revenue. As a legislator, how would you approach the public's desire for property tax relief with local revenue needs?
What role, if any, should the Wyoming Legislature play in economic development?
Explain your values when it comes to campaign donations. Does Wyoming's system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures need reform?
pageProps.questionnaireIntro:
The answers below were solicited from candidates via a written questionnaire created by WyoFile reporters and editors in June 2026. Responses are presented exactly as submitted, without fact-checking, wordsmithing or editing for grammar, punctuation or spelling errors. 

Legislative candidates were invited to respond to the questionnaire several times by email and by phone. Out of fairness to the candidates who met the deadline, WyoFile will not add responses after the guide's publication.
pageProps.aboutProject:
WyoFile produced this guide with coding and web development by Thomas Musselman, editing by Tennessee Watson and production assistance by Calla Shosh. Contact Tennessee Watson with questions, corrections or suggestions at <tennessee@wyofile.com>.

WyoFile is a Wyoming 501(c)3 nonprofit, independent, member-supported, public-interest news service reporting on the people, places and policy of Wyoming. WyoFile's [donor base](https://wyofile.com/supporters/) is politically diverse, including some donors who are candidates in this year's election. We maintain separation between sources of revenue and editorial direction, decisions and oversight. Acceptance of financial support does not constitute implied or actual endorsement of donors or their products, services or opinions, and does not confer editorial influence, nor does donor support imply any endorsement of WyoFile's reporting, commentary or editorial position.

This material is available for republication by select media outlets. Contact Collaborations Editor Rebecca Huntington at <rebecca@wyofile.com> to learn how.
